content.text: The Language Server Protocol (LSP) is a standardised JSON-RPC protocol that separates the language-intelligence logic of an IDE — go to definition, find all references, hover documentation, diagnostics — from the editor itself. A language server is a standalone process that understands one or more programming languages. Any client that speaks LSP can query it without knowing the internals of the language toolchain.

For a coding agent, LSP integration means gaining access to the same deep language understanding that powers modern IDEs: exact symbol definitions, all call sites of a function, type information at any position, and real-time diagnostics from the actual compiler or type checker. Rather than approximating these answers with text search, the agent gets ground-truth answers from a production-quality language analysis engine running against the same codebase.

Code navigation and understanding at scale requires structural knowledge that text search cannot provide. Finding all places that call a function is easy with ripgrep if the function has a unique name, but resolving which call site refers to this specific method (versus a different method with the same name on a different class) requires type information. LSP provides exactly this: type-resolved, semantically accurate answers to navigation queries.

LSP integration also gives the agent access to live diagnostics. Rather than running the type checker as a separate shell command and parsing its text output, the agent receives diagnostic messages directly from the language server as it edits, in a structured format with precise file locations. This enables a tighter edit-diagnose-fix loop without spawning subprocesses for every type-check invocation.
textDocument/definition resolves the exact file and line where a symbol is defined, correctly handling inheritance, imports, and re-exports where text search would find multiple false positives.
textDocument/references finds every call site, type annotation, or import of a symbol across the entire workspace, regardless of how it is expressed syntactically.

The server pushes textDocument/publishDiagnostics notifications whenever the document changes, giving the agent an immediate, structured list of errors and warnings without running a separate process.
textDocument/hover returns full type signatures, documentation, and deprecation notices for any symbol, giving the agent the same information a developer sees when hovering in an IDE.
workspace/symbol lets the agent find any class, function, or variable by name without knowing which file it lives in, across the entire workspace.

LSP integration has significant setup costs. Each language requires its own server (pyright or jedi-language-server for Python, rust-analyzer for Rust, typescript-language-server for TypeScript). The servers must be installed, started, and kept running for the duration of the agent session. A polyglot repository requires multiple servers running simultaneously. Startup time ranges from under a second (jedi) to several seconds (rust-analyzer indexing a large crate graph), adding latency to the first queries of a session.

LSP also assumes that the documents being analysed are properly opened and synchronised with the server. The agent must send textDocument/didOpen before querying a file, and textDocument/didChange whenever it edits one — otherwise the server will respond based on stale on-disk content. Managing this synchronisation state correctly is non-trivial and a common source of bugs in LSP client implementations.

LSP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 as its message format. There are two fundamental message types: requests (which have an id and expect a response) and notifications (which have no id and expect no response). The client — in our case, the agent's LSP wrapper — sends requests and notifications over a transport (stdio by default, TCP optionally). The server responds to requests and also sends notifications proactively, most importantly publishDiagnostics.

Every LSP session begins with an initialize handshake. The client sends an initialize request containing its capabilities (what protocol features it supports), and the server responds with its own capabilities. After receiving the response, the client sends an initialized notification to signal that it is ready. Only after this handshake can the client send document or workspace requests.

Document positions in LSP are zero-indexed and expressed as a line number and a character offset within that line. All coordinates are in this format: a textDocument/definition request takes a TextDocumentPositionParams specifying the file URI and the cursor position. The response is a Location (or array of Locations) with a file URI and a Range.

Document synchronisation is central to correct operation. When the agent opens a file, it sends textDocument/didOpen with the file's full content. When it edits the file in memory (without yet writing to disk), it sends textDocument/didChange with either the full new content (full sync) or a list of incremental edits (incremental sync). The server processes these notifications to keep its internal model current.

attrs.alt: Architecture showing the coding agent using a JSON-RPC client to communicate with a language server process over stdio, with capability groups (textDocument, workspace, codeAction, document sync) shown below.

content.text: The JSON-RPC client manages the message transport, request/response correlation (matching response ids to pending requests), and the notification dispatch loop. The language server runs as a separate OS process; stdio pipes serve as the transport. The agent's LSP tool layer translates high-level queries (find definition of this symbol) into LSP protocol messages and returns structured results.

Use LSP when the agent needs semantically accurate answers to navigation questions: where is this function defined (not just where does this string appear), what are all the call sites of this method (resolved by type, not just by name), what are the type errors in this file right now. These questions have unreliable answers from text search and reliable answers from LSP.

Use workspace/symbol when the agent needs to discover symbols by name without knowing their location. Use textDocument/hover when constructing explanations of API surfaces. Use publishDiagnostics to drive a type-check-and-fix loop without invoking the type checker as a subprocess on every iteration. Skip LSP for tasks that do not require language-structural information — file reading, shell execution, and text search are faster and simpler for tasks that do not need semantic precision.
Install the language server for the target language (e.g. pip install pyright for Python).
Start the language server as a subprocess with stdin and stdout as pipes.
Send the initialize request with client capabilities. Wait for the initializeResult response. Send the initialized notification.
For every file the agent will query, send textDocument/didOpen with the file's current content.
Run a background reader thread/coroutine that reads incoming messages, dispatches responses to waiting request futures, and queues notifications.
Expose tool functions (get_definition, get_references, get_hover, get_diagnostics) that the agent calls as tools.
On session end, send textDocument/didClose for open files and shutdown/exit notifications.

# Minimal async LSP client using asyncio subprocess
# pip install pyright  (installs the language server)
import asyncio
import json
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any


class LspClient:
    """Minimal JSON-RPC client for a stdio-based language server."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._proc: asyncio.subprocess.Process | None = None
        self._pending: dict[int, asyncio.Future] = {}
        self._next_id = 1
        self._reader_task: asyncio.Task | None = None
        self.diagnostics: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}

    async def start(self, command: list[str], root_path: str) -> None:
        self._proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            *command,
            stdin=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.DEVNULL,
        )
        self._reader_task = asyncio.create_task(self._reader())
        await self._initialize(root_path)

    async def _initialize(self, root_path: str) -> dict:
        result = await self._request("initialize", {
            "processId": None,
            "rootUri": Path(root_path).as_uri(),
            "capabilities": {
                "textDocument": {
                    "synchronization": {"didOpen": True, "didChange": True},
                    "definition": {"dynamicRegistration": False},
                    "references": {"dynamicRegistration": False},
                    "hover": {"dynamicRegistration": False},
                    "publishDiagnostics": {},
                }
            },
        })
        await self._notify("initialized", {})
        return result

    async def _send(self, message: dict) -> None:
        body = json.dumps(message).encode()
        header = f"Content-Length: {len(body)}\r\n\r\n".encode()
        self._proc.stdin.write(header + body)
        await self._proc.stdin.drain()

    async def _request(self, method: str, params: dict) -> Any:
        req_id = self._next_id
        self._next_id += 1
        fut: asyncio.Future = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()
        self._pending[req_id] = fut
        await self._send({"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": req_id, "method": method, "params": params})
        return await asyncio.wait_for(fut, timeout=10.0)

    async def _notify(self, method: str, params: dict) -> None:
        await self._send({"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": method, "params": params})

    async def _reader(self) -> None:
        """Background loop: read messages from the language server."""
        while True:
            header = await self._proc.stdout.readline()
            if not header:
                break
            if not header.startswith(b"Content-Length:"):
                continue
            length = int(header.split(b":")[1].strip())
            await self._proc.stdout.readline()  # blank line
            body = await self._proc.stdout.readexactly(length)
            msg = json.loads(body)
            if "id" in msg and msg["id"] in self._pending:
                self._pending.pop(msg["id"]).set_result(msg.get("result"))
            elif msg.get("method") == "textDocument/publishDiagnostics":
                params = msg["params"]
                self.diagnostics[params["uri"]] = params["diagnostics"]

    # ---- High-level helpers ----

    async def did_open(self, path: str) -> None:
        content = Path(path).read_text()
        await self._notify("textDocument/didOpen", {
            "textDocument": {
                "uri": Path(path).as_uri(),
                "languageId": "python",
                "version": 1,
                "text": content,
            }
        })

    async def get_definition(self, path: str, line: int, character: int) -> list[dict]:
        result = await self._request("textDocument/definition", {
            "textDocument": {"uri": Path(path).as_uri()},
            "position": {"line": line, "character": character},
        })
        if isinstance(result, dict):
            return [result]
        return result or []

    async def get_references(self, path: str, line: int, character: int) -> list[dict]:
        return await self._request("textDocument/references", {
            "textDocument": {"uri": Path(path).as_uri()},
            "position": {"line": line, "character": character},
            "context": {"includeDeclaration": False},
        }) or []

    async def get_hover(self, path: str, line: int, character: int) -> str | None:
        result = await self._request("textDocument/hover", {
            "textDocument": {"uri": Path(path).as_uri()},
            "position": {"line": line, "character": character},
        })
        if not result:
            return None
        contents = result.get("contents", "")
        if isinstance(contents, dict):
            return contents.get("value", "")
        return str(contents)

    async def stop(self) -> None:
        await self._request("shutdown", {})
        await self._notify("exit", {})
        if self._reader_task:
            self._reader_task.cancel()
The following example starts pyright, opens a Python file, and queries it for the definition of a symbol at a known position, then retrieves the hover documentation.

# Start pyright and query a Python file for definition + hover
import asyncio

async def demo():
    client = LspClient()
    await client.start(
        command=["pyright", "--stdio"],
        root_path="/home/user/myproject",
    )

    # Give pyright a moment to index the workspace
    await asyncio.sleep(2.0)

    src_file = "/home/user/myproject/src/auth.py"
    await client.did_open(src_file)
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)  # let diagnostics arrive

    # Find where the symbol at line 42, character 12 is defined
    definitions = await client.get_definition(src_file, line=42, character=12)
    for loc in definitions:
        uri = loc.get("uri", "")
        start = loc.get("range", {}).get("start", {})
        print(f"Defined at: {uri}:{start.get('line')}:{start.get('character')}")

    # Get hover info (type signature + docstring)
    hover = await client.get_hover(src_file, line=42, character=12)
    print(f"Hover:\n{hover}")

    # Print any diagnostics the server emitted for this file
    file_uri = f"file://{src_file}"
    for diag in client.diagnostics.get(file_uri, []):
        sev = diag.get("severity", 0)
        msg = diag.get("message", "")
        pos = diag.get("range", {}).get("start", {})
        print(f"[{sev}] {pos.get('line')}:{pos.get('character')} {msg}")

    await client.stop()

asyncio.run(demo())
The next example shows how an agent exposes LSP capabilities as tool definitions that the LLM can call to navigate and understand code.

A coding agent is asked to rename a method safely across a 50,000-line Python codebase. It first calls goto_definition to confirm the canonical definition location. It then calls find_references to get all call sites — 23 locations across 8 files. For each reference, it reads the file, makes the rename, and sends a textDocument/didChange notification. After all edits, it calls get_diagnostics on each modified file to confirm that pyright reports no errors. If any diagnostics appear, it reverts the problematic edit and reports the conflict to the user.

# Rename-and-verify using LSP references + diagnostics
async def rename_symbol(
    lsp: LspClient,
    file_path: str,
    line: int,
    character: int,
    new_name: str,
) -> str:
    """
    Find all references to the symbol at (file_path, line, character)
    and rename them to new_name, then verify with diagnostics.
    """
    await lsp.did_open(file_path)
    refs = await lsp.get_references(file_path, line, character)
    if not refs:
        return "No references found. Symbol may not exist."

    # Group by file
    by_file: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
    for ref in refs:
        uri = ref["uri"]
        by_file.setdefault(uri, []).append(ref["range"])

    edited_files: list[str] = []
    for uri, ranges in by_file.items():
        path = uri.removeprefix("file://")
        source = Path(path).read_text()
        lines = source.splitlines(keepends=True)

        # Apply renames from bottom up to preserve line offsets
        for rng in sorted(ranges, key=lambda r: r["start"]["line"], reverse=True):
            row = rng["start"]["line"]
            col_start = rng["start"]["character"]
            col_end = rng["end"]["character"]
            original_line = lines[row]
            lines[row] = original_line[:col_start] + new_name + original_line[col_end:]

        new_source = "".join(lines)
        Path(path).write_text(new_source)
        await lsp.did_open(path)  # re-open with new content
        edited_files.append(path)

    # Wait for diagnostics to arrive
    await asyncio.sleep(1.0)

    errors: list[str] = []
    for path in edited_files:
        uri = f"file://{path}"
        for d in lsp.diagnostics.get(uri, []):
            if d.get("severity") == 1:  # error
                errors.append(f"{path}:{d['range']['start']['line']}: {d['message']}")

    if errors:
        return "Rename introduced errors:\n" + "\n".join(errors)
    return f"Renamed {len(refs)} references across {len(by_file)} files. No errors."

The most common mistake is querying the language server before sending textDocument/didOpen. The server may respond with empty results or stale cached results for files it has never been told about. Always send didOpen before any query on a file, and track which files are open to avoid sending redundant open notifications.

A second common mistake is not waiting for diagnostics after making changes. publishDiagnostics is a push notification that arrives asynchronously after the server has processed the change; querying diagnostics immediately after sending didChange will read stale data. Use a short sleep or a proper notification callback to wait for the diagnostics cycle to complete.

Maintain a set of file URIs that have been opened. Before querying a file, check if it is already open; if not, send didOpen. If the file has been edited on disk since it was opened, send a fresh didOpen with the current content.
Use asyncio for the JSON-RPC client.

LSP communication is inherently concurrent: the agent sends requests while simultaneously receiving push notifications from the server. An async implementation handles this naturally without threading.
After initialize, most language servers need a few seconds to index the workspace. Add a brief sleep or wait for a workspace/semanticTokens/refresh notification before issuing navigation queries.
A textDocument/definition request on a built-in function or a dynamically defined attribute will return null or an empty array. The agent must not treat this as an error.
Send shutdown followed by exit to allow the server to flush any pending state. Killing the process directly leaves lock files and may corrupt the server's cache on disk.

LSP query latency varies by server and query type. Simple definition and reference queries typically return in 50-200ms after the workspace has been indexed. Hover queries are usually faster (10-50ms) as they only require type information for a single symbol. Workspace/symbol queries on large codebases can take 200-500ms depending on the number of symbols indexed.

Workspace indexing is the dominant startup cost. Pyright on a 50,000-line Python project takes 2-5 seconds; rust-analyzer on a medium Rust crate takes 5-15 seconds. For agent sessions that will issue many queries, this startup cost is quickly amortised. For single-query sessions, spawning a full language server is wasteful — use ctags or ripgrep instead.
Start the language server once per agent session and reuse the connection for all queries. Restarting the server for each query would re-incur the indexing cost every time.
LSP supports concurrent requests (they have unique ids and responses can arrive out of order). Issue multiple independent queries simultaneously rather than sequentially to reduce total latency.
Fast, full type inference, excellent diagnostics
Fast startup, lightweight, good for scripting
Wraps the TypeScript compiler; full type inference
Fast text lookups on any language
Symbol navigation without a running server

Language servers are long-running processes that may consume significant memory (500MB-2GB for large projects). The agent must monitor for server crashes and restart the session with proper re-initialization when they occur. Implement exponential backoff on restart attempts; repeated crashes indicate a misconfiguration or an unsupported project structure.

LSP servers should not be exposed to untrusted input. The agent should only open and query files that are within the trusted project root. An adversarial document that triggers a language server bug (buffer overflow in a native language server component like clangd) could compromise the agent's host process. Validate all file paths against the project root before passing them to the LSP client.
LSP gives coding agents access to the same language intelligence as modern IDEs: type-resolved definitions, complete reference lists, live diagnostics, and rich hover information.
The protocol uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio; every session begins with an initialize/initialized handshake before any document queries.
Document synchronisation is mandatory: send textDocument/didOpen before querying a file and textDocument/didChange after every edit, or the server will return stale results.
publishDiagnostics arrives asynchronously as a push notification; query diagnostics after a brief delay following an edit, not immediately after sending didChange.
Start the language server once per session and reuse the connection; the workspace indexing cost (2-15 seconds) makes per-query server restarts impractical.
Use LSP for semantically precise queries; fall back to ripgrep or ctags for quick lookups where exact type resolution is not needed.
Monitor server health and implement crash recovery; language servers can fail on malformed code or unsupported project configurations.
